prompt: As used in the passage, the word “triumphs” most nearly means

choices.rationale: No loud noises are meant.
Correct. He lists his small successes in the new city.
No crowd celebration is described.
The literal military sense does not fit 'small' personal wins.

stimulus.title: The Unsent Letter
stimulus.text: Tomas wrote the letter three times before he stopped trying to send it. In the first version he was cheerful, listing the small triumphs of his new city as if homesickness were a thing he had outgrown. In the second he was honest, and the page filled with everything he missed until it embarrassed him. The third version said almost nothing at all. He kept that one folded in a drawer. Months later he found it again and realized the words he could not send had still done their work: writing them had let him admit, if only to himself, how far from home he truly felt.

prompt: As used in the passage, the word “blunting” most nearly means

choices.rationale: The 'speaking plainly' sense does not fit.
Color dullness is not the sense here.
Correct. The grasses reduce the force of storms.
Cutting in half is not what the grasses do to waves.

explanation: To be 'blunting the force' is to reduce that force.

stimulus.title: The Quiet Buffer
stimulus.text: Where the land meets the sea, a salt marsh can look like wasteland: flat, muddy, and crossed by winding channels. Yet this drab landscape does remarkable work. Its dense grasses slow incoming waves, blunting the force of storms before they reach homes inland. The same grasses trap sediment, letting the marsh build itself upward as the sea slowly rises. Beneath the mud, roots store carbon that might otherwise warm the air. For years, marshes were drained and filled without much thought. Only later did people recognize that the unglamorous marsh had been protecting them all along, asking nothing in return.

prompt: As used in the passage, the word “hinge” most nearly means

choices.text: a sudden moment of doubt or hesitation
choices.rationale: No doubt or hesitation is meant.

choices.text: a crucial element on which something depends
choices.rationale: Correct. Zero is the crucial element on which modern math turns.

choices.text: a faint trace of a particular quality
choices.rationale: The 'faint trace' sense (a hint) does not fit here.

choices.text: a metal joint that lets a door swing
choices.rationale: The literal door-joint sense does not fit the abstract use.

explanation: The 'quiet hinge on which math turns' is a crucial element it depends on.

stimulus.title: The Number That Was Nothing
stimulus.text: For much of history, there was no symbol for nothing. People counted sheep and coins easily enough, but the idea of writing down an empty amount seemed pointless, even unsettling. The breakthrough came when mathematicians treated zero not as the absence of a number but as a number in its own right, one that could be added, subtracted, and used to hold a place in larger figures. With zero, the difference between 5 and 50 could be written clearly, and long calculations grew far simpler. A concept that once felt like a riddle became the quiet hinge on which much of modern mathematics turns.

prompt: As used in the passage, the word “snatch” most nearly means

choices.rationale: No complaining is meant.
Correct. The bat grabs an insect quickly from the air.
A brief glance does not fit the action described.
Tearing into pieces is not the sense here.

explanation: To 'snatch an insect from the air' is to grab it quickly.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: As used in the passage, the word “dissolved” most nearly means

choices.rationale: Correct. The fortunes faded away as spices became common.
Settling a dispute is not the sense.
No contract is being broken here.
No literal melting of fortunes occurs.

stimulus.title: The Price of Pepper
stimulus.text: There was a time when a handful of black pepper could rival a handful of silver. The spice grew only in distant lands, and by the time it reached a European market it had passed through many hands, each adding a markup. Merchants guarded their routes jealously and spread tall tales about how spices were harvested, partly to discourage rivals. The high prices drove sailors to seek their own sea paths to the source, voyages that redrew the map of the world. In the end, the very abundance those voyages produced made spices ordinary, and the fortunes built on rarity quietly dissolved.

prompt: As used in the passage, the word “grips” most nearly means

choices.rationale: No fear is involved.
Complaining is not the sense here.
Correct. The fungus holds firmly to the stone.
The 'comprehends' sense does not fit a fungus on rock.

explanation: The fungus that 'grips the stone' holds firmly to it.

stimulus.title: First on the Rock
stimulus.text: When a bare rock is left exposed, the first life to arrive is often a lichen, a strange partnership of fungus and alga living as one. The fungus grips the stone and gathers moisture; the alga, sheltered inside, makes food from sunlight. Together they need no soil at all. As a lichen grows, it slowly releases acids that crack the rock surface, and its dead tissue mixes with grit to form the first thin crumbs of soil. Years later, mosses and small plants can take root in that beginning. The lichen will likely be crowded out by the very community it made possible, an unsung founder of the green world that follows.

prompt: As used in the passage, the word “knit” most nearly means

choices.rationale: Correct. Locks bound distant towns together as trading partners.
The bone-healing sense does not fit this context.
No literal yarn is involved.
The 'furrowed brow' sense does not fit.

explanation: To 'knit towns into trading partners' means to bind them together.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.

prompt: As used in the passage, the word “record” most nearly means

choices.text: to set down in writing for the first time
choices.rationale: The noun sense, not the verb 'to record,' fits this use.

choices.text: a flat disc that plays music
choices.rationale: The musical-disc sense does not fit the context.

choices.text: a preserved account of past events
choices.rationale: Correct. The coral preserves an account of past seasons.
No achievement or 'best result' is meant here.

explanation: Here 'a record of past seasons' means a preserved account of them.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.
